Title: N9 polls: Pas instructs members to support BN candidates
Article:
TAMPIN: Pas has instructed its members to support Barisan Nasional (BN) candidates in the Negri Sembilan election, in a move reflecting closer cooperation between the two coalitions.
Pas secretary-general Takiyuddin Hassan called on party members in the Tampin parliamentary constituency to vote for both BN and Perikatan Nasional (PN) candidates contesting in three state seats in the Aug 1 polls.
"I want to call on and instruct all Pas members in the Tampin parliamentary constituency to come out and vote on Aug 1. Support our candidate from BN in Gemencheh, support our candidate in Gemas and do not forget the MCA candidate in Repah so that we can defeat Pakatan Harapan (PH) in that constituency," he said at the Gelombang Biru programme in Felda Bukit Rokan last night.
The
candidates
are PN's Ridzuan Ahmad in Gemas, BN's Suhaimizan Bizar in Gemencheh and BN's Koh Kim Swee in Repah.
Takiyuddin said the directive mirrored a similar instruction issued during the Johor state election, where Pas members were told to back BN candidates as part of a broader electoral strategy.
"The objective is to defeat PH by ensuring BN candidates win, regardless of whether they are from Umno, MCA or MIC. God willing, this effort will bring great benefits to Muslims, the Malay community and our beloved nation, Malaysia," he said.
Also present at the event were Parti Wawasan Negara president Hamzah Zainudin and Tampin Umno division chief Mohd Isam Mohd Isa.
Speaking to reporters later, Takiyuddin said the directive was binding on members of all PN component parties, not just Pas.
"Pas members and members of all PN component parties are bound by this directive. All have been instructed to support BN candidates and the coalition's agreed candidates in the Negri Sembilan election," he said.
He said the move underscored a united front between PN and BN aimed at wresting control of the state from Pakatan Harapan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil prices rise slightly after US announces new round of strikes on Iran
Article:
NEW YORK: Oil prices inched higher in early trading on Wednesday as fears of further supply disruptions intensified after US forces said it began striking Iranian military targets for the 11th straight night and Kuwait reported attacks by Iranian drones.
Brent crude futures rose 0.55 per cent, or 50 cents, to US$91.51 after markets opened on Wednesday, while US West Texas Intermediate crude climbed 0.36 per cenr, or 30 cents, to US$84.64 in low volume trading.
This comes after oil settled at a five-week high on Tuesday after US forces struck targets in southern and western Iran, while Iran attacked US facilities in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan.
The US military said it began its latest strikes on Iran late on Tuesday in the United States, or early Wednesday in Iran.
The US attacks came a short while after the Kuwaiti army said its air defences were intercepting Iranian drones on Wednesday.
The constant trading of strikes have raised fears of further disruptions to global energy supplies after Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis opened a new front in the Iran war by threatening to target vessels carrying Saudi oil in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait and announcing a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia.
The Bab el-Mandeb waterway at the southern entrance to the Red Sea has become an increasingly important route for Saudi crude exports as traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has fallen sharply since a ceasefire between the United States and Iran collapsed earlier this month.
Separately, Pete Hegseth, the US defense secretary said the country's war in Iran has cost it US$37.5 billion so far, an increase of nearly US$8 billion since the last public estimate.
Data from the American Petroleum Institute showed that US crude and distillate inventories rose last week, while gasoline stockpiles fell, market sources said.
The inventory data comes ahead of official figures from the US Energy Information Administration on Wednesday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hundreds flee as wildfire rips through southern France
Article:
COTIGNAC, France: A fast-moving wildfire erupted Tuesday in southern France, forcing hundreds out of their homes as nearly 1,000 firefighters and water-bombing aircraft battled to stop the blaze.
The fire swept through wooded hills in the Var region, burning 1,700 hectares in just a few hours and prompting the evacuation of nearly 400 people, the area's top official said.
"The fire is moving down Gros Bessillon mountain at a very fast speed, which was not at all anticipated given the climatic conditions and weakening wind," said Simon Barbre, prefect for the region located between Marseille and Nice.
He said evacuations took place around the villages of Cotignac, Montfort-sur-Argens and Correns. Many of those moved to safety came from Ponteves, where the fire started, and the nearby village of Sillans-la-Cascade.
Very dry conditions in the area have caused an extreme forest-fire risk, according to the French weather service Meteo-France.
Barbre said air crews were racing to lay down fire-retardant barriers ahead of the advancing flames.
An AFP journalist near Cotignac saw a huge plume of grey smoke billowing behind a ridge engulfed in flames.
"This fire is the absolute priority of the whole area right now," said Var fire chief Eric Grohin, adding that between 900 and 950 firefighters were deployed on the ground.
- 'MAJOR DISASTER' -
"It's grown enormously, it's spreading to other villages," said Isabelle Scarica, one of around 50 volunteers in Ponteves preparing sandwiches for firefighters.
She said the situation was "a major disaster."
One fire engine was damaged and four firefighters and two residents of Ponteves suffered minor smoke inhalation, according to local authorities.
Before aircraft were grounded for the night, firefighting crews carried out more than 100 water and retardant drops using eight Canadair water bombers, four-water-dropping helicopters and two Dash aircraft.
Fuelled by repeated heatwaves and drought, the 2026 wildfire season began earlier than usual, and firefighters have been battling near-daily outbreaks across France in recent weeks.
Another major blaze burned 200 hectares and destroyed homes in the Var region between Sunday and Monday near Les Arcs-sur-Argens.
Nearly 40,000 hectares have burned in France since the start of the year, "far more than was burned during the whole of last year", according to Interior Minister Laurent Nunez.
Wildfires were also active elsewhere in France.
In the Alps, a lightning-sparked blaze that began five days ago had burned 130 hectares in a remote area of the Ecrins mountain range.
On the Mediterranean island of Corsica, firefighters were still battling a blaze near Corte after nine days. --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Moroccan man's death during arrest sparks protests, probe in Italy
Article:
ROME: Morocco sought a "full clarification", and Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni on Tuesday called for "the truth right to the end" after a Moroccan man died during an arrest, igniting protests.
The tragedy occurred after the man was tackled to the ground by police.
"Regarding the death of Abderrahim Fakir, it is imperative that any potential causes are thoroughly investigated with the utmost rigour," she said.
"The truth must be pursued without prejudice or leniency for anyone," Meloni wrote in a post in which she also condemned violence during a protest in Bologna over the death.
Morocco's foreign ministry said it had asked the Italian authorities to "fully clarify" the death and determine responsibility and take appropriate legal measures.
The ministry said it was following the case "very closely."
On Monday, prosecutors opened an inquiry into the death of the 42-year-old man, who had come to Italy at the age of five.
He died after being restrained by police chest-first on the ground for several minutes.
In a video taken by a resident, Fakir is seen calling for help while pressed to the ground by two policemen and watched by other first responders.
The two policemen and the four first responders are under investigation by the prosecutor.
A demonstration with several thousand people took place on Monday in Bologna, according to images from local media.
Clashes broke out with law enforcement, leaving 64 officers injured, according to the police headquarters.
News channel Sky TG24 reported that eleven protesters were also injured.
Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi called for waiting for the results of the investigation, telling the daily Il Messaggero that "no one is above the law."
The embassy of Morocco in Italy said "it is in the interest of everyone that full light be shed on this tragedy, by establishing the full circumstances of the events with rigour, impartiality and transparency."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'PBN' coined to reflect PN-BN pact in Negri polls, says Hamzah
Article:
TAMPIN: Perikatan Nasional (PN) has coined the acronym "PBN" to describe its electoral understanding with Barisan Nasional (BN) in the Negri Sembilan state election.
Parti Wawasan Negara president Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin said the label was intended to avoid confusion among voters.
He said the name was merely a convenient way to distinguish the cooperation between the two coalitions, stressing that the partnership was driven by a shared goal of forming the next state government.
"We decided to call it PBN because people kept referring to PN and BN separately. The name itself is not important.
"What matters is our shared spirit and commitment to determine Negri Sembilan's future," he said after attending the Ceramah Gelombang Biru programme at Felda Bukit Rokan last night.
Also present were PN secretary-general Datuk Seri Takiyuddin Hassan, former Bersatu vice-president Datuk Seri Dr Ronald Kiandee, Tampin Umno division chief Datuk Mohd Isam Mohd Isa, Gemencheh BN candidate Suhaimizan Bizar, and Gemas PN candidate Ridzuan Ahmad.
Hamzah said PN had agreed from the outset for BN to lead the electoral pact in Negri Sembilan, adding that both sides had set aside past differences in the interest of providing a better future for the state.
"Naturally, there were feelings arising from previous issues, but these can be resolved through discussions.
"That is why we met early, so that after this everyone can focus on campaigning and explaining to voters what we intend to do for the people of Negri Sembilan."
Hamzah said growing support for the coalition suggested voters were embracing what he described as a "blue wave" against the ruling coalition.
"If the blue wave continues to gain the support of the majority of the people, God willing, we will take over the Negri Sembilan government," he said.
Asked whether the PN-BN cooperation in Negri Sembilan could pave the way for an early general election, Hamzah said a victory by the alliance would demonstrate that governments could be changed through the democratic process.
"If we succeed in replacing the current government with PBN, it will give confidence to Malaysians that dissatisfaction can be resolved through democracy."
When asked whether "PBN" would be formally registered as a political entity, Hamzah said the matter had yet to be discussed with coalition leaders.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: At least 53 dead in Guyana capsized ferry disaster
Article:
GEORGETOWN, Guyana: At least 53 people died after a ferry capsized off the coast of Guyana over the weekend, Prime Minister Mark Phillips said Tuesday.
The MV Barima was traveling from the capital Georgetown to Port Kaituma in Guyana's inland Essequibo region when it flipped over on Saturday night.
A desperate search for survivors began early Sunday, with 76 people rescued so far, according to Phillips, after the government previously said 77 had been located alive.
The prime minister on Tuesday revised the death toll for the second time in one day, raising it from 41 to 53.
"A total of 53 bodies have been recovered," Phillips told a press conference.
"We've recorded 76 persons that were rescued as of 6pm (2200GMT) today," he said.
There had earlier been confusion over how many were involved in the tragedy due to an inaccurate passenger manifest which listed 133 people aboard, Public Works Minister Juan Edghill said.
But on Monday, Phillips said he was confident that 179 people had been on board when the accident occurred.
Two crew members aboard the aging ferry tested positive for marijuana after the incident, he told reporters Sunday, adding that an investigation was underway.
The ship began its journey in mid-afternoon and a distress call was received about eight hours later.
The ferry was built in 1939, according to the shipping website VesselFinder.
Boats typically travel by sea before reaching a river to arrive at Port Kaituma, which is located in the depths of a dense rainforest.
Guyana, a small English-speaking country at the northeast corner of South America, is locked in a territorial dispute with neighbouring Venezuela over the oil-rich Essequibo region.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: N9 polls: PN-BN pact sealed four days before nomination day, says Takiyuddin
Article:
TAMPIN: Perikatan Nasional secretary-general Datuk Seri Takiyuddin Hassan revealed that the understanding between the coalition and Barisan Nasional (BN) for the Negri Sembilan state election was finalised just four days before nomination day.
The Pas secretary-general said the agreement was concluded by himself, Parti Wawasan Negara (Wawasan) president Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin, Umno secretary-general Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki, and Negri Sembilan Umno leaders.
Takiyuddin described the understanding as a historic turning point in Malaysian politics, marking the start of closer cooperation between the two coalitions.
"We should have been working together and standing united to defend our country a long time ago.
"However, as mentioned, that effort was delayed for certain reasons.
"What we have achieved during the Negri Sembilan state election is an important milestone.
"Datuk Seri Hamzah, Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi, the Negri Sembilan Umno leadership and I finalised this understanding four days before nomination day.
"This is PN and BN — one united front that, God willing, can no longer be separated," he said during the Ceramah Gelombang Biru programme at Felda Bukit Rokan last night.
Also present were former Bersatu vice-president Datuk Seri Dr Ronald Kiandee, Tampin Umno division chief Datuk Mohd Isam Mohd Isa, Gemencheh BN candidate Suhaimizan Bizar, and Gemas PN candidate Ridzuan Ahmad.
Takiyuddin, who is also the Kota Baru member of parliament, said the move by both coalitions' leadership was aimed at restoring ties and strengthening relations between Umno and Pas.
He recalled that the two parties had previously sought to rebuild ties through Muafakat Nasional following Barisan Nasional's defeat at the federal level.
"As Datuk Isam mentioned, we once tried to restore the brotherhood between Umno and Pas.
"On Sept 14, 2019, after the Barisan Nasional government was defeated at the federal level, Pas and Umno launched an alliance known as Muafakat Nasional.
"For the first time, some Pas members stepped into Dewan Merdeka at the Putra World Trade Centre, Umno's headquarters.
"There were tears because we wondered why it had taken so long for us to come together. We should have united much earlier," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nike to tighten online sales in China amid 'cluttered' marketplace
Article:
NEW YORK: Nike is trying to lure back shoppers in China by controlling how its products are sold online, directing consumers to official Nike channels as the American sportswear giant continues to lose ground to domestic rivals.
By restricting wholesale distributors' online sales, the company aims to rebuild trust with Chinese shoppers and sell its products at full price, said Cathy Sparks, vice president and general manager of Greater China.
Starting in January, key sportswear retailers in China will stop selling Nike's clothing and shoes online and will instead pivot to in-store sales, Sparks told Reuters. Online, the company's products will be sold through new Nike-branded digital storefronts on the popular Chinese e-commerce platforms Tmall, JD.com and Douyin, and Nike's website and app.
"Our marketplace has become so fragmented and cluttered," said Sparks, a 25-year company veteran who was appointed to oversee Chinese operations earlier this year. "What consumers want is an experience that's premium, true to the brand, trustworthy, and certainly connected between digital and physical."
CHINA SALES DECLINE PERSISTS
China, Nike's third-largest market, remains a key source of concern for the world's biggest sportswear brand. The e-commerce shift is part of a broader effort to revive growth.
Sales in Greater China fell 17 per cent on a ‌constant-currency basis ⁠in the fourth quarter, the company reported last month, steepening from a 10 per cent decline in the previous quarter.
Fast-rising domestic rivals Anta and Li Ning have chipped away at Nike's market share, while foreign brands like On and Hoka have also surged.
Nike's China woes have reinforced for investors that CEO Elliott Hill's turnaround strategy still faces significant obstacles. In his nearly two years at the helm of the company, Hill has pushed to refocus on ⁠sports, rebuild wholesale relationships in North America and introduce new products.
The majority of Nike's 16 store partners in China, which own and manage thousands of Nike stores, will stop selling online, a Nike spokesperson said.
Following local news reports about the potential e-commerce change in June, BNP Paribas senior analyst Laurent Vasilescu said the move would be a "strategic misstep" and would hand opportunities to competitors.
"Nike doesn't have a distribution problem in China and elsewhere. It has a product problem," Vasilescu said in a research note.
Releasing products that are more relevant to Chinese consumers is also among Nike's priorities, Sparks said. The company has appointed a vice president of local product creation in Greater China, she added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US judge blocks Trump administration from stripping immigrants of work permits
Article:
BOSTON: A federal judge on Tuesday temporarily blocked President Donald Trump's administration from stripping tens of thousands of asylum seekers and immigrants who have Temporary Protected Status of their ability to work in the United States.
US District Judge Nathaniel Gorton in Boston sided with a coalition of immigrant rights groups and labour unions that sued to block US Citizenship and Immigration Services from moving forward with a series of policies designed to implement new immigration-related restrictions enacted by Congress last year.
Gorton's order will be in place until he decides whether to issue a longer-term pause of the Trump administration's policy. The judge said he would rule on that by Aug 5 USCIS did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Trump's signature tax and spending law that was passed by the Republican-led Congress in July 2025 for the first time imposed fees to apply for asylum and restricted employment authorization for people with Temporary Protected Status, or TPS.
That is a designation that lets migrants from countries stricken by ​war, natural disaster or other catastrophes live and work in the United States ​while it is unsafe for them to return to their home countries.
The Trump administration has sought as part of the Republican president's hardline immigration agenda to end TPS for people from more than a dozen countries. The US Supreme Court last month allowed it to do so in the case of thousands of Haitian and ⁠Syrian immigrants.
The plaintiffs in a lawsuit filed by the liberal legal group Democracy Forward argued that USCIS had unlawfully implemented the new law's provisions and that its policies needed to be halted, particularly one that would lead to thousands of TPS holders from El Salvador, Sudan and Ukraine potentially losing their ability to work starting on Wednesday.
Among those policies are ones that the plaintiffs say wrongly cut short the time TPS holders would be authorized to work by retroactively applying the new restrictions to people from El Salvador, Sudan and Ukraine.
Skye Perryman, Democracy Forward's president and CEO, said in a statement that Gorton's ruling ensures that thousands of families will not lose their livelihoods while courts consider whether the administration's policies are legal.
Democratic former President Joe Biden's administration extended TPS for people from El Salvador, Sudan and Ukraine in January 2025. TPS remains valid for El Salvador through Sept 9 and for Sudan and Ukraine through Oct 19.
The plaintiffs argued the new USCIS policies were invalid because the public never received notice and a chance to comment on them before they were adopted, as required by the Administrative Procedure Act, and applied the 2025 law's TPS work authorization provisions retroactively without statutory authorisation.
Gorton on Tuesday declined to block USCIS from collecting the fee for now, but said the agency cannot strip people who fail to pay it of work permits or impose other penalties.
The case was filed in Boston, a venue popular among litigants challenging Trump's agenda, and landed before one of the few judges on the court who was not appointed by a Democrat, Gorton, an appointee of Republican President George H.W. Bush.--REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MAA lifts 2026 car sales forecast as EV, SUV demand accelerates
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The country's vehicle market has defied market expectations, prompting the Malaysian Automotive Association to lift its 2026 sales forecast after a stronger first-half performance.
MAA had raised the full-year total industry volume (TIV) to 800,000 units from 790,000 units, its president Mohd Shamsor Mohd Zain told a briefing on Tuesday.
The improved sales outlook is boosted by policy clarity from the extended timeline for the New Customised Incentive Mechanism (NCIM), Shamsor added.
This has given Malaysia's automotive industry more confidence to plan production, investments and sales strategies, MAA added.
The industry recorded a TIV of 385,353 vehicles sold in the first six months of 2026, up three per cent from 373,636 units a year earlier, driven by continued appetite for sport utility vehicles (SUVs) and rising adoption of electrified vehicles.
MAA's revised forecast comprises 744,000 passenger vehicles and 56,000 commercial vehicles, compared with its earlier projections of 730,000 and 60,000 units respectively.
Despite the upgrade, the forecast remains below the industry's record 2025 TIV of 820,752 units, reflecting a more cautious outlook for the second half.
MAA has also raised its electrified vehicle sales forecast, expecting all EV sales to reach 120,000 units, accounting for 15 per cent of total industry sales, compared with its earlier estimate of 100,000 units.
Battery electric vehicles (BEVs) and hybrid vehicles are each expected to contribute 60,000 units.
The stronger EV outlook comes as manufacturers expand their electrified model line-ups, while SUV demand continues to drive market growth.
MAA said SUV sales rose 19 per cent, supported by new model launches and Proton's EV offerings, while sales of all electrified vehicles surged 69 per cent during the first half.
National Brands Strengthen Grip
National automakers continued to dominate the market, with the combined market share of Perodua and Proton rising to 67 per cent, or 256,304 units, compared with 63 per cent a year earlier.
Perodua accounted for 41 per cent of the total market, while Proton contributed 25 per cent. Non-national brands made up the remaining 33 per cent, or 129,049 units.
On the production front, total industry production increased 1.2 per cent to 356,946 units in the first half from 352,626 units previously.
While passenger vehicle demand remained resilient, MAA said the commercial vehicle segment continued to face challenges.
Sales declined 11 per cent, particularly among pick-up trucks, following the withdrawal of diesel subsidies for private registrations. The move affected demand and contributed to lower production in some vehicle categories.
Policy Clarity Supports Outlook
MAA said the government's decision to further defer the implementation of NCIM and P.U.(A) 402 until Dec 31 this year provides manufacturers and distributors with more time to prepare their operational plans, investment commitments and market strategies.
P.U.(A) 402 refers to the Excise Regulations (Determination of the Value of Locally Manufactured Goods for the Purpose of Imposing Excise Duty).
In simple terms, it is a regulation that determines how the value of locally-assembled or manufactured vehicles is calculated for excise duty purposes.
This valuation affects how much excise duty vehicle manufacturers and assemblers pay, which in turn can influence vehicle pricing.
The NCIM, currently being refined by the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry, is expected to introduce a more targeted incentive framework.
It will focus on strengthening localisation, technology transfer and higher-value automotive activities, including automotive electronics, software development, battery technologies and advanced driver assistance systems.
Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani previously said the revamped mechanism aims to simplify the incentive structure while encouraging foreign manufacturers to deepen local supply chains and undertake higher-value activities in Malaysia.
MAA said the extended timeline would allow industry players to better adjust to the upcoming framework, while giving the government more time to engage automakers and stakeholders on an implementation structure that strengthens Malaysia's automotive ecosystem.
Shamsor said discussions with the ministry on the NCIM have been constructive, with continued engagement between industry players and the government.
He said the additional six-month extension would allow more consultation to ensure the new mechanism balances localisation goals, domestic ecosystem development and industry competitiveness.
MAA vice president Alagasan Gadigaselam said the industry supports efforts to increase local content, strengthen vendor development and encourage technology transfer.
"Many locally manufactured components incorporated imported raw materials or materials specified by global original equipment manufacturers, but they still generate significant Malaysian value through engineering, manufacturing, processing, tooling, et cetera.
"Our objective is to work with the government to ensure the implementation framework is practical, commercially viable, and supports continued investment and long-term industry competitiveness."
Meanwhile, Shamsor said MAA has received assurance from the government that any eventual changes arising from the review of existing automotive incentive arrangements, including P.U.(A) 402, are expected to have little or no impact on vehicle prices.
He added that authorities are still refining the calculation methodology to account for different business models across the industry and have committed to providing sufficient lead time before any changes take effect.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Canada cancels joint bridge celebration with US, citing Trump trade threats
Article:
OTTAWA: Canada has canceled a planned joint celebration with the US to mark the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge between Windsor, Ontario, and Detroit, citing trade threats from President Donald Trump.
"In light of trade action threatened by the United States earlier this week, it would be inappropriate to proceed with a celebratory event between the two countries," Jenna Ghassabeh, a spokesperson for Canadian Infrastructure Minister Gregor Robertson, said in an emailed statement.
The bridge is scheduled to open to traffic on July 27. A ribbon-cutting ceremony was planned for July 24 with US and Canadian officials expected to attend, Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens said in interviews with local media.
Canada is still "committed" to opening the bridge on July 27 but will mark the milestone by celebrating "among Canadians" on July 24, Ghassabeh said. On Monday, Trump unveiled 50pct tariffs on a wide range of imports from Canada in response to ​what the US administration called its discriminatory treatment of American-made cars, alcohol and dairy goods.
The US$4.7-billion bridge, named after the legendary Detroit Red Wings hockey player, had originally been set to open in June. In February, Trump threatened to block the project, citing Canada's refusal to stock some US alcoholic beverages, its tariffs on dairy products and its trade talks with China. The bridge opening was allowed to proceed after the US and Canada struck a deal on tolls this month.
The new bridge will help ease truck traffic on the Ambassador Bridge into Detroit, the US-Canada border's largest freight port, which handled US$126 billion in trade carried by commercial trucks in 2023. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump: US airlines can now fly to Lebanon for first time since 1985
Article:
NEW YORK: President Donald Trump said on Tuesday, after meeting with Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun, that he was directing the US government to allow all domestic airline carriers to fly directly to that country.
All US flights were suspended in 1985 after the hijacking of TWA Flight 847 from Athens, Greece, en route to Rome, Italy.
The flight was forced to land in Beirut. During the 17-day ordeal, a US Navy diver was killed. Before flights to Lebanon could resume, US officials will need to conduct an extensive security review of the Beirut airport and certify its safety.
It was unclear if US airlines would be willing to resume flights. The major US airlines, American Airlines, United Airlines and Delta Air Lines, have halted flights to nearby Tel Aviv, citing security concerns.
The FAA said it "looks forward to working with airlines and our interagency partners to implement the President's directive and support the safe return of flights to Lebanon." The US State Department currently has a "Do Not Travel" advisory for Lebanon for Americans, citing high tensions in the Middle East.
In February, the government ordered non-emergency US government employees and family members to leave Lebanon, due to safety risks.
Trump ordered the US Transportation Department to lift a ban on US passenger air service to Venezuela in January after the US  captured the country's president, Nicolas Maduro, in a military operation that month.
Service resumed in late April after seven years, with an ​American Airlines flight from Miami to Caracas. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump slaps 100pct tariff on imported generic drugs from 2028
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump announced on Tuesday the imposition of steep tariffs on imported generic drugs from August 2028, a move he said was aimed at onshoring production of such pharmaceutical products.
"Effective August 1st, 2026, all Generic Drugs being brought into the United States will continue to have a TARIFF of ZERO PERCENT for a two year period of time, after which the TARIFF will be raised to 100% for a one year period of time, and 200% thereafter," he said in a post on his Truth Social platform.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US war in Iran has cost US$37.5 billion so far: Pentagon
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States' war in Iran has cost US$37.5 billion so far, US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Tuesday, an increase of nearly US$8 billion since the last public estimate.
Hegseth told lawmakers at a hearing in Washington that the figure included certain aspects of the war as well as anticipated costs through September 30.
It ⁠was unclear how the Pentagon arrived at the US$37.5 billion figure. A source told Reuters in March that ⁠President Donald Trump's ​administration estimated the first six ​days of the war had cost at least US$11.3 billion.
Hegseth also told lawmakers that military training would need to be curtailed without an urgent funding boost.
This is the first time that Hegseth, who appeared before the Senate Appropriations Committee along with Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, General Dan Caine, has publicly faced questions from lawmakers since the US military re-started operations against Iran earlier this month.
With just six months before midterm elections in which Trump's Republicans may face an uphill battle to keep their House majority, Democrats are riding high in public opinion polls as they attempt to link the unpopular Iran war with affordability.
A fragile ceasefire between the United States and Iran collapsed earlier this month and both sides have been carrying out daily strikes against each other.
The number of US troops killed in the war on Iran rose to 17 over the weekend, while officials are examining the remains of another soldier missing following a strike on a base in Jordan. Two of the 17 were confirmed to have been killed when Iran attacked ​US personnel in Jordan.
Roughly 430 US troops have been injured so far in the conflict, which started in late February. The Pentagon said on Monday that 100 service members were injured since July 7, but that 96pct have returned to duty. It was unclear how ​many of those, if any, were included in the 430 number in the Pentagon's public database.
In the past week, Trump has threatened to expand ​the targets being ⁠struck in Iran to include energy plants and bridges, send ground forces to seize Iran's Kharg Island oil hub and bomb a deep-underground nuclear-linked site known as Pickaxe Mountain.
On Tuesday, Trump said the US would take out the mountain facility "pretty soon." --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump set to hit dozens of nations with new tariffs
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States is set to impose new tariffs that could hit dozens of countries soon, trade envoy Jamieson Greer signaled Tuesday, with President Donald Trump's temporary global levies due to expire this week.
The Trump administration has prepared fresh tariffs targeting 60 trading partners over their alleged failures to act against forced labour, as officials push to rebuild the US leader's trade agenda after legal setbacks.
"We expect to see some action soon," Greer told CNBC when asked if new duties were incoming. He did not specify a timeline.
While Trump imposed a 10-per cent global duty this year after a swath of his tariffs were struck down by the Supreme Court in February, this levy expires Friday.
Analysts expect that new tariffs over forced labour concerns – set between 10 per cent and 12.5 per cent – would replace these temporary duties.
They come as Trump makes a renewed push to use tariffs as leverage against US trading partners, sparking fears of retaliation and heightened tensions.
Washington announced a fresh 25-per cent duty on certain Brazilian goods last week, and on Monday unveiled a 50-per cent levy on many Canadian products to take effect in 30 days.
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said Tuesday that he was looking at "all options," adding that he and Trump had agreed to "intensify discussions" in the coming weeks on a possible deal.
- FORCED LABOUR CONCERNS -
Greer said Tuesday that new action on forced labour will cover the majority of US trade.
"The US has laws to prohibit trading goods with forced labour. Other countries, most don't have a law, those that do don't really enforce it," he said.
But these moves could reignite trade tensions.
A 10-per cent tariff rate would hit US imports from partners including Canada, the European Union, Mexico, Taiwan and the United Kingdom. They were found to have taken steps against forced labour.
Goods from over 40 other major economies like China, India and Japan face a 12.5 per cent levy.
The EU previously said that it considers tariffs imposed on these grounds "unjustified."
- CANADA PRESSURE -
Washington's planned 50-per cent tariff on Canada also comes as US-Mexico talks over a North American free trade pact intensify.
Washington recently declined to extend the accord in its current form.
Greer is set to travel to Mexico from Wednesday to Friday for discussions linked to a joint review of the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA).
But negotiations with Canada have proceeded at a slower pace. Carney on Tuesday did not suggest that he would head to Washington for talks.
Some lawyers see Trump's use of an untested legal provision – Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930 – as a means to gain leverage over Canada in USMCA negotiations.
Trade lawyer Dave Townsend of Dorsey & Whitney added that higher tariffs "appear to be aimed at encouraging an agreement between Canada and the United States, or in retaliation for the failure to reach such agreement, or both."
The question, he said, is whether both sides will start a "cycle of escalation and retaliation."
Crucially, Trump's latest salvo will not exempt affected Canadian products entering his country under the USMCA.
Trump told reporters Tuesday that the Canada tariffs were unrelated to his earlier threats over wildfire smoke that descended into the United States.
- BRAZIL TENSIONS -
US plans for a 25-per cent tariff on Brazilian goods over accusations of unfair trade practices have separately drawn a sharp rebuke from the Latin American giant.
The levy is due to take effect Wednesday, while shaping up as a major campaign flashpoint just months before Brazil's presidential election.
A range of products like beef, coffee and certain aircraft parts will be exempted, as will some goods that the United States does not produce.
Still, the American Chamber of Commerce for Brazil recently warned that Washington's measure places Brazil among countries "facing the most restrictive conditions for access to the US market," affecting more than US$11 billion in exports.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MRSM students turn fish bones into calcium supplement
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
SIX students and a biology teacher from Maktab Rendah Sains Mara (MRSM) Pasir Salak have developed a calcium supplement using the bones of the suckermouth catfish or known locally as 'ikan bandaraya'.
The innovation aims to tackle the spread of the suckermouth catfish (Hypostomus plecostomus), an invasive South American species that breeds rapidly and disrupts river ecosystems by outcompeting native fish for food and space.
MRSM Pasir Salak biology teacher Mahfuza Mansor said the idea stemmed from research conducted with students two years ago using chicken bones.
"Following a five-month research, we successfully processed chicken bones into calcium supplement powder," she said, adding that the project went on to win a gold medal at the national-level Idea'24 competition in September 2024.
Mahfuza said she decided to apply the same concept to the invasive fish species when discussions about its ecological threat gained traction on social media.
The concept turned into a structured research project in April last year under the Schoolwide Enrichment Model (SEM), an initiative within the MRSM education system aimed at fostering higher-order thinking and creative productivity.
"We spent almost three months studying research materials. We found the fish bones to contain calcium, collagen and gelatin, and have the potential to be turned into health supplements," said Mahfuza.
Specimens were sourced from social media content creator Mohd Nur Badri Mat Sidek, or Dunia Badol on Facebook, who frequently catches the invasive fish.
After repeated trials, a stable calcium powder prototype was produced and sent to Universiti Teknologi Petronas for composition analyses.
"Test results showed high calcium levels, while arsenic levels were extremely low at 0.008mg per litre — well below the maximum permissible limit of 5mg per litre," said Mahfuza.
The project won a gold medal at the International Invention and Innovation Competition in Jakarta last month.
It also won a gold medal and the Excellent Research Award at the Expo-Sciences International in Abu Dhabi last year.
Team leader Muhammad Danish Iman Ahmad Daud, 16, said time management and project deadlines were major challenges.
Teammate Muhammad Rayyan Zafri Che Amat, 16, said handling chemical procedures with precision was vital as minor errors could alter research findings. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'MCMC has blocked 3.04b suspicious texts since 2022'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC), in collaboration with telecommunications service providers, blocked a total of 3.04 billion suspicious text messages and 2.5 billion scam calls between Jan 1, 2022 and June 30 this year.
Communications Minister Datuk Seri Fahmi Fadzil said 190,429 mobile and fixed lines that sent suspicious text messages were terminated in the same period to curb fraud.
"MCMC also takes regulatory and enforcement action under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 and Online Safety Act 2025," he said in a written parliamentary reply to Datuk Seri S. Vell Paari.
"MCMC monitors harmful online content under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act, while the Online Safety Act empowers the commission to act against content involving financial fraud," he added.
Between Jan 1 and July 7 this year, MCMC received and processed 158 complaints on scam calls, including those impersonating government agencies, through the Consumer Redress Portal.
He said the public should report suspected scam numbers, websites and accounts to service providers and the authorities, while victims of financial fraud should contact the National Scam Response Centre hotline at 997 to block the movement of funds.
On another matter, Fahmi said social media platforms were adhering to Malaysia's ban on users under 16 but had requested more time to fully comply with the Risk Mitigation Code and Child Protection Code.
"We see good signs from these platforms. They want to make sure they do not misunderstand what is intended and have asked for more time.
"We have granted it. The cooperation at this time is good," he said after launching the 2026 International Regulatory Conference.
Fahmi warned that licensed platforms that failed to comply with the gazetted codes could face court action and fines of up to RM10 million.
Malaysia began enforcing the ban on new social media account registrations for users under 16 on June 1.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Why Hormuz, Malacca and key maritime straits matter in global conflicts
Article:
PARIS: Hormuz and a handful of maritime straits around the world have served as the linchpin of global trade since the 16th century and have fuelled geopolitical rivalries throughout history.
As Iran tightens its grip on the Strait of Hormuz, its Yemeni allies, the Houthi rebels, want to disrupt shipping in the Red Sea to and from Saudi ports and could possibly disturb traffic in another strait, Bab el-Mandeb, which ships must cross to reach the Suez Canal, experts say.
These two passages, which lock access to the Gulf and to the Red Sea, are among the dozen or so straits considered vital to international trade.
Oil and gas tankers, bulk carriers and container ships loaded with raw materials and goods pass through these narrow natural corridors.
The United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea signed in Montego Bay on December 10, 1982 – a kind of constitution for the oceans – "formalises the fact that a strait is a natural feature that must not be subject to tolls" and where "the principle of free navigation" is guaranteed, Sylvain Domergue, a lecturer at Sciences Po Bordeaux and author of Geopolitics of Maritime Spaces told AFP.
By contrast, canals such as Panama or Suez, which are man-made waterways, may be subject to tolls levied by the states that control them.
In Hormuz, Iran, which "has not ratified the Montego Bay Convention, had until now respected it under customary law," Domergue said.
Portugal, whose navigators were the first to open the sea route to the Indies, tried as early as 1507 to establish control over Hormuz to secure a monopoly on access to spices and the riches of the Orient, he said.
- 'THE SEA BELONGS TO NONE' -
But after a famous controversy, the Mare Liberum thesis of Dutch jurist and philosopher Hugo Grotius prevailed in the early 17th century: straits were then seen as essential to long?distance international trade because "the sea belongs to no one."
"To guarantee freedom of navigation, one had to start by guaranteeing freedom of passage through the straits," Domergue said.
The Netherlands adopted this doctrine and defended freedom of the seas, followed by Britain and later by the United States which to this day respects "almost all of the Montego Bay Convention," despite not having ratified it either, he said.
Yet the principle of free navigation "did not prevent the British from guiding their colonisation in part according to the geography of straits", Domergue said.
Britain's key military footholds were established along strategic waterways. Its great colonial naval base in Asia would be in Singapore, allowing control of Malacca, through which the largest share of global maritime traffic from China now passes.
London would also establish bases on the island of Socotra, near Bab el-Mandeb and the entrance to the Red Sea.
In Europe, the Danish straits (the Oresund, the Kattegat), which close off the Baltic, "were of great importance in the 19th century because they enabled the Danes, for a very long time, to collect a toll," and are strategic because they "block access to the Atlantic for the Baltic powers, particularly the Russians," the geographer said.
- TURKISH EXCEPTIONS -
The only major straits where a toll is still collected are the "Turkish Straits" of the Bosphorus and the Dardanelles, which are governed by earlier agreements, signed in Montreux in 1936.
"In the long run, other issues concerning straits will arise," Domergue said, citing the Taiwan Strait, given China's declared territorial ambitions in Southeast Asia, or the Strait of Malacca, shared by Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia.
"There is already a 'war of sand' going on in the Taiwan Strait: Chinese vessels come to collect marine sand for the construction sector in China, in waters claimed by Taiwan-provocative actions," he said.
On the evening Iran announced plans to introduce a toll in Hormuz, "the Malaysian prime minister spoke out to say it was a very good idea that he might emulate for Malacca, before backtracking," Domergue noted.
"If there were a toll in Malacca, Singapore would lose some of its splendour and its status as a hub of East Asia," he added. --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stratus targets bigger order book
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Newly listed Stratus Global Holdings Bhd expects to double its order book within the next 12 months.
Executive director and chief executive officer Ryo Narisawa said the company had emerged from a relatively slow period and was seeing stronger interest from major customers.
"We have gone through a dry period, but we are now in active discussions with major existing and new customers," he said during a virtual press conference.
Narisawa added that the stronger pipeline was expected to lift the group's revenue in the next financial year, if not earlier.
"Within the next 12 months, we are looking to boost our order book by at least two times and possibly up to four times."
On the new manufacturing facility, Narisawa said Stratus Global had signed a sale and purchase agreement for a property next to its existing Penang facility, with the expansion expected to be completed by 2028.
He said the new facility would increase the group's manufacturing space from 117,000 sq ft currently to between 170,000 sq ft and 200,000 sq ft.
Stratus Global made a strong debut on Bursa Malaysia's Main Market, rising nearly 145 per cent above its IPO price of 80 sen.
The stock opened at RM1.96, a premium of RM1.16, with 55.1 million shares changing hands in early trade.
Narisawa said the opening price was higher than expected, describing the listing as a major milestone that gives Stratus Global more resources to accelerate its expansion plans.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN: Scam gangs cost Asia Pacific US$88b in 2025
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
SCAM gangs defrauded Asia Pacific residents of at least US$88 billion last year, said a United Nations office on Tuesday, with transnational crime groups rapidly evolving tactics to stay ahead of law enforcement.
Criminals in Southeast Asia, an epicentre for cyberfraud by mostly Chinese-origin syndicates, are outpacing crackdowns, diversifying revenue streams, exploiting corruption and harnessing artificial intelligence, said the UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) in a new report.
The report estimated scam losses across East Asia, Southeast Asia, Australia and New Zealand at between US$88.3 billion and US$114.1 billion for 2025, a figure larger than the gross domestic product of several of the nations in the region.
Networks that once operated within specific territories or illicit trades are increasingly expanding across multiple illegal markets while using shared services, said Delphine Schantz, UNODC regional representative for Southeast Asia and the Pacific.
"Their operating model looks like corporate franchising: imagine specialised departments for laundering money, trafficking people, smuggling migrants and harvesting data, all plugged into the same, service-based interconnected network," she said.
Scam centres in Southeast Asia run illegal online schemes that defraud people worldwide, generating billions of dollars of annual revenue. Many facilities are staffed by trafficked foreign nationals, confined in oppressive environments.
People from at least 80 countries and territories have been identified in scam compounds across the region, with recruitment networks using transit hubs in Asia, the Middle East and Africa, according to UNODC.
Scam operators were increasingly seeking recruits from outside Asia as they expanded into new markets, said UNODC.
Recruitment advertisements linked to the industry's communication channels had targeted people with German, Polish, Dutch, Spanish, Italian, French, Swedish, Norwegian and English language skills, it added.
Recent pressure from law enforcement in the region had displaced but not dismantled operations, said UNODC, with gangs relocating from countries like Myanmar and Laos to Timor-Leste, Pacific Island states, and parts of Africa.
Syndicates engage in "jurisdiction shopping" to find places with weak governance and regulation, said the report, adding that corruption was the "primary enabler" of tech-driven organised crime in the region.
The scam sector's rapid growth had fuelled an ecosystem of ancillary criminal services, including money laundering, underground banking and data trading, it said. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump warns US 'not finished' with Iran as Gulf conflict widens
Article:
TEHERAN: President Donald Trump warned Tuesday that the United States was "not finished" attacking Iran, as Teheran widened strikes on Gulf states after its Houthi allies threatened to blockade Saudi ports.
Two weeks after fighting resumed between the decades-old foes, Trump said Iran would need more than 20 years to recover from the damage inflicted in the Middle East war.
"If we left right now, it would take Iran 20, 25 years to rebuild," he said. "We're not finished at all... we're not leaving right now."
The warning came as Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain reported Iranian attacks, and as Trump used a White House meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun to pledge support for his fragile effort to disarm Hizbollah and move toward peace with Israel.
Bahrain said it intercepted Iranian attacks as sirens sounded repeatedly, while Kuwait and Jordan said they had intercepted drone and missile strikes.
Kuwait said Teheran had hit power and water desalination plants over several days, including on Monday, forcing residents to ration energy.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards confirmed a strike on Jordan, saying they had targeted a "complex housing US terrorist forces in the region of Rukban."
The Iranian army had earlier said it targeted US assets in Kuwait and Bahrain, including air defence systems, radar installations and administrative buildings.
"With this radar sweep, the enemy should prepare for even more decisive and powerful waves of drone and missile attacks," the Guards said in a statement carried by state news agency IRNA.
- HOUTHI THREAT -
Trump said he would "take care of" Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels if they followed through on a threat Monday to blockade Saudi Arabia's ports.
"If something like that happens, we take care of it," Trump said. "We've done that with the Houthis before, and we haven't heard from them in a while since we did what we did originally."
Saudi Arabia strongly condemned the threat from what it called the "terrorist Houthi militia."
It remains unclear how the Houthis would enforce such a blockade.
But maritime intelligence provider Vanguard Tech said a Singapore-flagged tanker and a Liberia-flagged tanker both "reversed course" in the Red Sea after loading crude at the Saudi port of Yanbu.
If the Houthis make good on the threat, they could endanger Riyadh's ability to bypass the Strait of Hormuz for some oil exports, with major repercussions for the global economy.
Saudi Arabia and the Houthis traded fire last week for the first time in years, threatening a 2022 truce, but the rebels have largely remained on the sidelines since Israel and the United States attacked Iran in late February.
The Revolutionary Guards said Tuesday that two tankers attempting to transit the strait "were hit by explosions, resulting in massive fires that brought them to a standstill."
The UK Maritime Trade Operations agency earlier reported that an "unknown projectile" struck a tanker off the coast of Oman.
- COST OF WAR -
Following the deaths of at least three US soldiers in recent days, Washington launched new attacks against Iran on Monday that it said were "designed to further degrade" Iranian capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
The deaths bring to 17 the number of US service members killed since the war resumed on July 7. Nearly 100 have been wounded, the Pentagon said Monday.
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told US senators Tuesday that the war has cost the United States US$37.5 billion so far, up from a previous estimate of nearly US$29 billion in mid-May.
In Iran, Shahin, a 34-year-old embroiderer from Iranshahr, said: "We're still struggling to secure our most basic life necessities, and now we're dealing with destruction from the war on top of it."
Despite the fighting, Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said diplomatic exchanges with Washington through mediators were continuing.
Mediator Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif called for restraint during a meeting with Iran's Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni.
Sharif urged all sides to "refrain from actions that could further destabilise the region", his office said.
Trump also sought to show US support for Lebanon, whose army has begun deploying in southern areas under a US-backed framework agreement with Israel aimed at extending state authority and disarming Iran-backed Hizbollah.
Meeting Aoun at the White House, Trump said Lebanon had been "hit hard" for decades but promised: "We're going to help it. We're going to help it a lot."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hundreds battle new mega-wildfire in Spain
Article:
TAMAJON: More than 600 firefighters and troops and 29 aircraft on Tuesday battled for a sixth day a huge wildfire north of Madrid that authorities said is one of the most difficult they have faced.
The blaze, which broke out on Thursday in the province of Guadalajara around 100 kilometres north of Madrid, is considered the most significant wildfire in Spain this year.
Some 1,200 people have been evacuated from their homes and the flames have so far scorched 30,000 hectares of land, officials said.
A thick column of smoke rose above the mountains surrounding Tamajon, a small village of around 150 residents where firefighters had established their command centre, according to an AFP journalist at the scene.
Helicopters and planes took turns dropping water on the flames.
Castilla-La Mancha regional president Emiliano Garcia-Page said firefighters had "practically stopped" the blaze from advancing toward the town of Soria but it was still not under control.
"It is the most complex of those that have been tackled in recent years in Spain," he told reporters.
The fire broke out in an isolated forest area and quickly spread across neighbouring hills, fuelled by strong winds.
Authorities said it had been difficult to control because the area is sparsely populated, with large stretches of abandoned scrubland, and severe drought conditions.
Spain entered its third heatwave of the summer on Tuesday, with temperatures forecast to rise above 40C across large parts of the country, further complicating firefighting efforts.
Firefighters said that 34 isolated hamlets had been evacuated across Castilla-La Mancha.
On top of 1,200 people ordered out of their homes, people in 14 other districts had been told to stay indoors away from the flames.
Earlier this month, Spain suffered one of its deadliest wildfires in recent history when a blaze in the southern province of Almeria killed 13 people and destroyed around 7,000 hectares.
"We are going through particularly complex weeks, and the risk of fires remains extremely high," government spokeswoman Elma Saiz told a news conference.
Scientists say human-caused climate change is increasing the length, intensity and frequency of heatwaves, drying out vegetation, and raising the risk of destructive wildfires.
In 2025, more than 393,000 hectares were destroyed by fire, according to the European Forest Fire Information System – the most in Spain's recent history.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Aminuddin: Zahid's remarks a challenge to Anwar's authority
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Negri Sembilan Pakatan Harapan chairman Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun has described Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi's remarks challenging Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim to sack Barisan Nasional ministers as inappropriate.
Free Malaysia Today reported that Aminuddin said Zahid's statement appeared to challenge the prime minister's authority.
"If that was indeed what he (Zahid) said, I believe it was a challenge.
"I do not think it was appropriate for (such a statement) to be made by a friend and close ally of the prime minister," he was quoted as saying on Tuesday.
Aminuddin said Anwar's statement was merely a reminder to ensure ties within the unity government remained intact.
He said the issue was not about dismissals but about respecting the spirit of cooperation and partnership within the federal government.
Aminuddin also claimed tensions between PH and BN in Negri Sembilan could recur at the federal level if disagreements continued.
On Sunday, Anwar had warned that ministers, deputy ministers, and heads of federal agencies
must resign
if they used their positions to attack fellow unity government partners during state election campaigns.
In response, Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan had said he was
ready to step down
from his cabinet post if ordered to do so by the prime minister.
On Monday, Zahid had said Anwar
can dismiss ministers
from the coalition if he is unhappy with their performance.
He had said there was no reason for BN ministers to resign voluntarily.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested for killing brother during argument
Article:
KUALA KUBU BARU: Police on Monday arrested a man for killing his sibling during a heated argument here.
Hulu Selangor police chief Superintendent M. Manimaran said the suspect, 26, was ordered by the magistrate's court to be placed under seven-day remand from today.
"We received a report about 5.30pm of a fight between siblings in Kuala Kubu Baru in which one of them was killed. The victim, 29, was confirmed dead at the scene," he said in a statement.
Manimaran said checks showed that the suspect has a criminal record with three offences, adding that urine tests conducted on him turned up negative for drugs.
He said police were still investigating the motive behind the killing and the cause of the argument.
The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder, which carries the death penalty or between 30 and 40 years' jail.
If not sentenced to death, the offender will also be given at least 12 strokes of the rotan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global growth could near zero over next century - PMO adviser
Article:
IPOH: Global economic growth could slow to near zero over the next century as rising trade barriers, intensifying geopolitical competition and supply chain disruptions reshape the global economy, Economic Adviser to the Prime Minister's Office Nurhisham Hussein said.
He said the changing environment requires countries, including Malaysia, to integrate national security considerations into broader economic policymaking to strengthen resilience against external shocks.
"So we are in a situation where I believe we're looking at global growth dropping to zero within the next 100 years. Obviously that's way past our time, but that is also the time of our children, grandchildren and great-grandchildren, so it's something that should concern us because it concerns the future of not just countries, but also humanity," he said.
Nurhisham was speaking as a panellist during the session 'The Economy: Development And Cooperation' at the 8th World Conference on Islamic Thought and Civilisation (WCIT), themed 'Everyone Matters: Pride and Unity in Islam', here today.
He pointed out that while Malaysia benefits from strategic geographical advantages along the Strait of Malacca and a diversified economy, the country still faces significant supply chain dependencies in critical raw materials and food production inputs.
Nurhisham highlighted that Malaysia relies heavily on a single foreign source for nearly 100 per cent of its potash supply and 60 per cent of its coal, while its self-sufficient poultry sector remains reliant on imported chicken feed from countries like Brazil and Argentina.
"So looking at it from a policy perspective, I think one of the things that we're doing now is that we're inserting a national security perspective into economic policy, and that's something that is both necessary and very worrying.
"Because when you talk about adding economic security to what is essentially a free trade construct, you're adding cost, you're adding to prices, right? You're having to accept inefficiencies for the sake of robustness, so that is a price that eventually has to be paid by consumers and that implies, again, slower growth," he added.
He said strengthening energy security, food security, industrial independence and supply chain resilience would remain among Malaysia's key policy challenges over the next two decades, particularly as geopolitical competition intensifies.
Meanwhile, another panellist, former Bank Negara Malaysia Governor Tan Sri Dr Zeti Akhtar Aziz said Islamic finance could play a greater role in supporting resilient economic growth because every financial transaction is backed by underlying economic activity, making the system less vulnerable to financial market disruptions.
"I have said at Islamic finance forums that we need to see breakthroughs, we don't want to see incremental growth. And why is it to our benefit? This is because Islamic finance, every financial transaction has an underlying economic transaction, it will drive economic growth," she said.
Zeti, who is also the founding chair and co-chair of the Asia School of Business, said the profit-sharing structure, transparency and disclosure inherent in Islamic finance make it an effective financial intermediation model to cushion the global economy against financial instability. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Add 'harms test' to FOI Bill to protect public interest, says Malaysian Bar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Bar has urged the Parliamentary Special Select Committee (PSSC) to overhaul the proposed Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill.
Its president Anand Raj said the bill focuses excessively on procedures for obtaining information held by public authorities, even though such processes could be implemented through internal guidelines and standard operating procedures without legislation.
"The bill must include a 'harms test', requiring public officers to assess whether withholding information would cause greater harm than the public interest served by disclosure before rejecting a request.
"All refusals must also be accompanied by written reasons," he said in a statement today.
Anand said Clause 12, which sets out exemptions from disclosure, was overly lengthy and should be narrowed to comply with permissible restrictions under the Federal Constitution.
He said the bill's broad exemptions, absence of a mandatory duty for proactive disclosure, and continued protection of official secrets would significantly limit public access to information.
He also called for the removal of offences under the bill which provides penalties of up to RM100,000 in fines, five years' imprisonment, or both for misuse of information, supplying false information and obstructing disclosure.
"These sanctions are excessively punitive, and the prospect of facing such severe penalties may deter requests for information.
"They are wholly incompatible with the purpose of the bill, which should promote greater openness and transparency, not impose more restrictive laws," he said.
Anand also urged amendments to remove unnecessary restrictions on who may request information.
He said the bill placed oversight responsibilities on "the Ombudsman", despite the body not yet being established.
Instead, he said the legislation should establish an independent oversight body with binding decision-making powers, authority to inspect records and compel disclosure, as well as safeguards to ensure independence in its appointments and funding.
"Passing the FOI Bill in its current form would be a disservice to the country.
"Malaysia deserves an effective and robust Freedom of Information Act that recognises freedom of information as a constitutional right, protects whistleblowers, enables public participation in governance and strengthens investigative journalism.
"We urge the PSSC to overhaul the Bill and deliver a meaningful Freedom of Information Act for Malaysia," he said.
On July 13, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the Bill would be referred to the PSSC for further scrutiny.
She said the move would allow for a more comprehensive review of every clause through engagements with members of Parliament from both sides of the divide and various stakeholders.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: School bullying - Time for accountability, not excuses
Article:
ASUCCESSION of bullying cases, culminating in a Form One student suffering a fractured ankle and the revelation that a disabled teenager dropped out of school after enduring relentless taunts, underscores the persistence of a problem that Malaysia has struggled to eradicate despite repeated promises and campaigns.
Bullying is no longer merely a disciplinary issue but a test of the education system's ability to provide a safe environment for every child.
Those who are tasked with putting an end to bullying in schools must be reminded that education never happens in an unsafe learning environment.
Every effort spent in teaching and learning in such a dangerous environment will surely go to waste. What is worse, such  harmful places of learning drive students out of the education system as has happened to the disabled teenager. She wasn't the first.
Many questions need answers, one of which is why wasn't the school aware of the relentless taunts endured by the disabled teenager?
Another is, how is it possible for the school authorities to not know about the three long months of bullying the Form One student suffered? For schools to be impervious to noticeable effects of bullying must surely have a chilling outcome on parents and students.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's warning against schools concealing bullying incidents to protect their reputation is significant. His warning acknowledges a long-standing concern that institutional defensiveness can sometimes take precedence over students' welfare.
The heads of schools that Anwar's warning is directed at must realise that the greater danger is not that schools report bullying cases. The greater danger is that they fail to report them, minimise them, or discourage complaints in order to avoid scrutiny.
As Anwar pointed out, swift action is essential to safeguard victims. Delaying, minimising or not reporting bullying incidents at all has serious consequences, the gravest of which is loss of life.
It isn't something that happens in some distant land. It has happened in Malaysia, not once but too many times. Loss of one life to bullying is one too many.
We do not know if any of the reasons was applicable to the bullying of the Form One student or the disabled teenager, but scrutiny is a must if we want the welfare of students to take precedence over institutional reputation, especially one that is built on a false image. Let's not build a house of cards.
The question is no longer whether bullying exists in Malaysian schools. It clearly does. The question is whether institutions are prepared to confront it openly and consistently.
Every high-profile case attracts public outrage, but lasting change requires a willingness to acknowledge uncomfortable realities rather than treat each incident as an isolated aberration.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Monthly assembly at night reflects round-the-clock policing, says KL's top cop
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kuala Lumpur police's decision to hold its monthly assembly at night reflects the reality of policing a capital city that never sleeps.
City police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said the initiative, held for the second time, underscored the force's 24-hour operational readiness, with most crime cases, intelligence-gathering, investigations and enforcement operations in the federal capital taking place after dark.
"Kuala Lumpur operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week, and the night is among the busiest periods in a metropolitan city like this. If we look at crime distribution by time, most cases are recorded at night.
"There is no difference between carrying out official duties, intelligence work, investigations, operations or even assemblies during the day or at night because policing in Kuala Lumpur is a continuous 24-hour responsibility," he told reporters after the monthly assembly tonight.
Fadil said organising the assembly at night was also aimed at strengthening the force's culture of night-time operational readiness, in line with the demands of policing the capital.
"We want to send a clear message that Kuala Lumpur will never sleep. Holding assemblies at night reinforces the active working culture required of our personnel because many policing duties are carried out during those hours," he said.
He added that the night assembly did not disrupt operational duties but instead provided the public with a clearer picture of the force's continuous commitment to maintaining security at all hours.
About 1,100 senior officers, rank-and-file policemen and civilian staff attended the event, which also featured the 70th anniversary celebration of the Kuala Lumpur Police Volunteer Reserve.
The assembly also saw the handover ceremony for the Kuala Lumpur Criminal Investigation Department chief's post from Senior Assistant Commissioner Mohd Razali Mohd Idrus to Assistant Commissioner Zulkarnain Samsudin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Turkiye's opposition leader to form new party to challenge Erdogan
Article:
ANKARA: Turkiye's opposition leader, Ozgur Ozel, said on Tuesday he will resign from the main opposition party and form a new one, amid a nearly two-year long legal crackdown on President Tayyip Erdogan's political opponents.
The move, which was expected, poses the latest challenge for the Republican People's Party's (CHP), which modern Turkiye's founder Mustafa Kemal Ataturk had established more than a century ago.
Most CHP voters and parliamentarians are expected to follow Ozel to the unnamed new party, after a court in May stripped him of his title as chairman by annulling the CHP's 2023 congress.
In a fiery speech in parliament, Ozel said the resignation of his team from the CHP did not mean they were succumbing to political pressure, but rather that they were embarking on a new kind of opposition to Erdogan, who has run Turkiye for more than two decades.
"Today, we are on the brink of a sad farewell that has become inevitable and whose time has come. However, we would like to share with the people that we carry the hope of a new beginning," Ozel said to more than 80 CHP lawmakers.
The May court decision cited irregularities in the CHP congress and reinstated as chairman Kemal Kilicdaroglu, the former leader who lost to Erdogan in the 2023 presidential election.
Ozel and critics called the ruling a politically motivated "coup" amid an unprecedented legal crackdown on the CHP that saw Istanbul Mayor and Erdogan's main political rival, Ekrem Imamoglu, arrested last year.
The government denies such assertions, saying the judiciary is independent. But the crackdown has rattled financial markets and fuelled concerns about democracy and the rule of law in Turkiye.
The opposition's crisis could boost Erdogan's prospects of extending his rule of the Nato-member country in an election scheduled to be held by 2028, but which analysts say could come earlier if the government seeks to take advantage of the CHP strife.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bystander wounded near White House to sue Secret Service, says lawyer
Article:
WASHINGTON: Lawyers for a bystander wounded in a shootout between the US Secret Service and a man outside the White House in May said they would file a personal injury lawsuit against the federal agency on Tuesday.
Benjamin Del Real Jr, 25, of Kansas, was on leave from active US Army duty on May 23 and walking with his sister and father when shots rang out in front of the White House, according to a statement from Clifford Law Offices.
Del Real, who was shot in the abdomen, said the gunman never faced him but Secret Service agents fired many rounds in his direction across a busy intersection, the law firm said.
Del Real underwent life-saving surgery at a local hospital, it said.
A gunman who fired at a White House checkpoint that day was shot by officers and died after being taken to hospital, the Secret Service said at the time.
The Secret Service statement said a bystander was struck by gunfire, but it did not make clear how badly the person was hurt or provide other details.
"Certainly it is the job of Secret Service agents to protect the president and other dignitaries, but that duty must be carried out with reasonable regard for the safety of innocent members of the public," lawyer Joseph Murphy said in the statement.
"Ben and his family had every right to be there. They had no body armour, no protection, and no warning before Ben was struck by gunfire."
Notice of the lawsuit, to be filed under the Federal Tort Claims Act, requests that evidence including firearms, shell casings and surveillance video be preserved, the statement said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MDEC to revoke digital status of Forest City school operator NSO Malaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 22 — The Malaysia Digital Economy Corporation (MDEC) is taking immediate steps to revoke the Malaysia Digital status of NSO Malaysia Sdn Bhd.
NSO operates Network School in Forest City, Iskandar Puteri, Johor.
MDEC said in a statement yesterday that the action follows the Iskandar Puteri City Council’s (MBIP) decision to revoke NSO’s business licence.
Conditions attached to the Malaysia Digital status require that a company obtain the relevant permits and licences to carry out its approved activities, and to comply with all applicable regulations and licensing requirements, MDEC said.
“The revocation of NSO’s business licence constitutes a breach of this requirement.
“Accordingly, MDEC is taking immediate action to revoke NSO’s Malaysia Digital status,” it said.
At the same time, MDEC said it welcomes high-quality digital investments into Malaysia, but stressed that full compliance with Malaysian laws and regulations cannot be compromised.
MBIP had earlier issued a Notice to Cease Business Operations to NSO effective July 22, 2026, following non-compliance with licensing conditions and the use of premises under the local authority’s jurisdiction.
There were allegations on social media that Network School was linked to an Israeli agenda to establish a foothold in Malaysia through cryptocurrency-related investment. — Bernama

Title: Petaling Jaya’s leasehold dilemma: High premiums, shrinking buyers and ageing leases
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 21 — Leasehold property owners in Petaling Jaya, one of the largest and oldest cities in Selangor, continue to grapple with an enduring legacy dilemma.
Initially set up as a satellite township, the early parts of Petaling Jaya were developed on rubber estate land, some of which was acquired by the state government and later alienated under leasehold titles.
Currently, leasehold property owners in Petaling Jaya have two options:
Option 1: A property owner can pay a nominal fee of RM5,000 and apply to extend the lease up to 99 years.
After extending the lease in this manner, the owner can only transfer the property to his or her spouse or biological or legally adopted children.
Option 2: Should the property owner intend to sell the property, he or she has to renew the lease for 99 years and pay the full premium based on this formula:
[¼ x 1/100 x Value of land x 99 years x Area of Land]
The property owner is entitled to a 30 per cent discount, but the premium must be settled within six months after Form 5A for lease renewal is issued.
However, many residents lament that the arrangement — especially the nominal fee option — risks becoming a financial trap for property owners, particularly senior residents.
Rajesh Mansukhlal, 70, who has lived in Section 6 for nearly 60 years, said the high premiums force many aged residents to either dip into their retirement savings or sell their properties below market price.
Rajesh said many property leases in Sections 1 to 18 would expire in about 42 years, with some properties having only 20 or 25 years remaining.
“Banks generally refuse loans for properties with leases between 20 to 25 years or less,” Rajesh, who is also the Kawasan Rukun Tetangga Section 6 deputy chairman, told Malay Mail.
